title: American Beef Stew
subtitle: Hearty beef and vegetable stew with potatoes, carrots, and a thick, savoury broth
description: American beef stew is a stovetop or oven braise of beef chuck with potatoes, carrots, celery, and onion — thicker and starchier than its French counterpart, with a beef-and-vegetable broth that is genuinely thick enough to coat a spoon. It is the home-cooking beef stew of diners and family tables across the United States, direct and satisfying. The flour-dusted beef browns in fat, the vegetables go in mid-cook, and the whole thing can be on the table in under two and a half hours.
sourceNotes: American beef stew is a foundational recipe of American home cooking, present in Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and a fixture of American cookbooks throughout the twentieth century. The technique of thickening with flour-coated beef and potato starch is distinctly American, contrasting with the wine-based French braise.

recipeTools.notes: A wide, heavy-based casserole or Dutch oven with a lid — oven-safe if you prefer to finish in the oven.

glossaryTerms.definition: The browned, sticky residue left in a pan after searing meat. Deglazing with liquid dissolves it into the broth, adding depth and colour.

ingredients.notes: Cut into 4 cm cubes — chuck is ideal for long braising
Cut into 3 cm chunks — added after 45 minutes
Cut into 3 cm pieces — added after 45 minutes

steps: Season the beef and toss with the flour to coat. Heat the oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown the beef in batches for 4–5 minutes per batch, turning to colour all sides. Remove to a plate.
Reduce to medium heat. Cook the onion and celery in the remaining fat for 5 minutes. Add the garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute.
Add the Worcestershire sauce and pour in the hot stock, scraping up the fond from the base. Add the thyme and bay leaves. Return the beef to the pot.
Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on the lowest heat (or at 160 °C fan in the oven) for 45 minutes.

Add the potatoes and carrots. Cover and cook for a further 45–55 minutes until the beef is completely tender and the potatoes are soft. The broth will have thickened from the flour on the beef and the starch from the potatoes.
Remove the thyme and bay leaves. Taste and adjust seasoning. Scatter with parsley and serve.

content.text: Browning the beef properly is the flavour investment. Do it in batches — one layer at a time, high heat, without moving the beef until a crust has formed. Crowded beef steams rather than browns and produces a grey, mild-tasting broth. The
that builds up in the pan through each batch is dissolved into the broth with the stock: a significant contributor to the stew's colour and depth.

Season {{beef-chuck}} of beef and toss with {{plain-flour}} of flour. Heat the oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Brown in batches, 4–5 minutes per batch. Remove. Cook onion and celery in the remaining fat for 5 minutes, then add garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute. Add Worcestershire sauce and pour in {{beef-stock}} of hot beef stock, scraping the base. Return the beef, add thyme and bay leaves. Cover and simmer for 45 minutes.

Add {{waxy-potatoes}} of potato chunks and {{carrot}} of carrot pieces. Cover and cook for 45–55 minutes more until the beef is completely tender and the vegetables are soft. Remove the herbs, taste and adjust seasoning, scatter with parsley, and serve.
